map.condition: Finding tuples common to two relations
Expressing division (R ÷ S) with primitive operators
Counting tuples after natural join with FD B->A, B->C
Checking if a SQL subquery returns any row

formulas.name: Cardinality and Degree
formulas.plain: Degree = number of attributes; Cardinality = number of tuples
formulas.whenToUse: When a question asks about size or structure of a relation

terms.means: number of columns (attributes)
number of rows (tuples)

formulas.trap: Confusing degree (columns) with cardinality (rows) — GATE often asks 'cardinality after join' meaning row count, not column count.

formulas.name: Cross Product Size
formulas.whenToUse: Computing the maximum number of tuples before applying a join condition

terms.means: number of tuples in relation R
number of tuples in relation S

formulas.trap: Cross product is NOT the result of a join; a join further filters with a condition and can yield fewer tuples.

formulas.name: Key and Superkey Definition
formulas.latex: K \text{ is a superkey of } R \iff K \rightarrow R; \quad K \text{ is a key iff no proper subset of } K \text{ is a superkey}
formulas.plain: K is a superkey if K -> R; K is a key if no proper subset of K is also a superkey (minimality)
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying candidate keys, primary keys, or checking minimal superkeys

terms.means: set of attributes forming the key
full set of attributes of the relation

formulas.trap: A primary key is ONE chosen candidate key; all candidate keys are minimal superkeys. A superkey need not be minimal.

formulas.name: Selection (sigma)
formulas.plain: sigma_condition(R) = all tuples t in R satisfying condition
formulas.whenToUse: Filtering rows based on a predicate (like WHERE in SQL)

terms.means: selection operator
boolean predicate on tuple attributes

formulas.trap: Selection does NOT remove duplicate columns — it works row-wise only. The output has same schema as R.

formulas.name: Projection (pi)
formulas.plain: Pi_{A1,...,Ak}(R) = project R onto attributes A1...Ak (duplicates removed)
formulas.whenToUse: Keeping only specific columns and eliminating duplicates (like SELECT DISTINCT in SQL)

terms.means: projection operator
list of attribute names to keep

formulas.trap: Projection automatically removes duplicate tuples. SQL SELECT without DISTINCT does NOT remove duplicates — a key distinction in GATE.

formulas.name: Set Operations
formulas.latex: R \cup S,\quad R \cap S,\quad R - S \quad (\text{require union-compatible schemas})
formulas.plain: R union S, R intersect S, R minus S — require same arity and compatible attribute domains
formulas.whenToUse: Combining or comparing two relations with the same schema

terms.means: union — tuples in R or S (duplicates removed)
intersection — tuples in both R and S
set difference — tuples in R but not in S

formulas.trap: Union compatibility requires same NUMBER of attributes with compatible domains — attribute names need not match exactly.

formulas.name: Intersection via Difference (non-primitive to primitive)
formulas.plain: R intersect S = R - (R - S)
formulas.whenToUse: Expressing intersection using only set-difference (and union), which are the primitive operators

terms.means: tuples in R that are not in S

formulas.trap: GATE frequently asks 'minimum operators from {union, difference} to express intersection' — the answer is 2 difference operations (no union needed).

formulas.name: Division Operator (R ÷ S)
formulas.plain: R / S = Pi_A(R) - Pi_A( (Pi_A(R) x S) - R )  where A = attrs(R) - attrs(S)
formulas.whenToUse: Finding tuples in R that are associated with ALL tuples in S (universal quantification / 'for all')

terms.means: attributes of R not in S (the 'quotient' attributes)
the divisor relation (subset of R's attributes)

formulas.trap: Division is derived, not primitive. GATE asks to express it with primitives — requires cross-product, set-difference, and projection (minimum 3 primitive operators).

formulas.name: Rename Operator (rho)
formulas.latex: \rho_{S(B_1,\ldots,B_n)}(R) \equiv \text{rename relation } R \text{ to } S \text{ and attributes to } B_1,\ldots,B_n
formulas.plain: rho_{S(B1,...,Bn)}(R) — rename R to S with new attribute names
formulas.whenToUse: Needed for self-joins or when combining two copies of the same relation

terms.means: rename operator
new relation name

formulas.trap: Without renaming, a self-join (e.g., finding pairs of employees in same dept) is ambiguous — always rename one copy.

formulas.name: Theta Join and Equi-Join
formulas.plain: R join_theta S = sigma_theta(R x S)
formulas.whenToUse: Joining two relations on a general condition theta (equi-join when theta is equality)

terms.means: join condition (predicate on attributes of R and S)
join operator symbol

formulas.trap: Theta join / equi-join retains BOTH join columns in the output; natural join removes duplicate join columns.

formulas.name: Natural Join
formulas.plain: R natural-join S = join on all common attributes, keeping each common column once
formulas.whenToUse: Joining on all attributes with the same name, automatically merging them

terms.means: union of attribute sets (common attrs appear once)

formulas.trap: If R and S have NO common attributes, natural join degenerates to cross product. If all attributes are common, it becomes intersection.

formulas.name: Natural Join Size Bounds
formulas.plain: 0 <= |R natural-join S| <= |R| * |S|
formulas.whenToUse: Bounding the number of tuples resulting from a natural join

terms.means: cardinality of the natural join result

formulas.trap: When a FD B->A holds and B is the join attribute, the max is min(|R|,|S|) because matching is 1-to-1 on B side. GATE 2014 classic question.

formulas.name: Outer Join Types
formulas.latex: \text{Left outer: } R \mathbin{⟕} S; \quad \text{Right outer: } R \mathbin{⟖} S; \quad \text{Full outer: } R \mathbin{⟗} S
formulas.plain: Left outer join: all R tuples + matched S (NULLs for unmatched); Right outer: all S tuples; Full outer: all tuples from both
formulas.whenToUse: When unmatched tuples from one or both sides must appear in the result (padded with NULLs)

terms.means: placeholder for missing attribute values from unmatched tuples

formulas.trap: Full outer join result can have MORE tuples than either relation alone. Inner join loses unmatched tuples from BOTH sides.

formulas.name: Lossless vs Lossy Decomposition
formulas.plain: Lossless: original relation = join of projections; Lossy: join produces spurious tuples (more than original)
formulas.whenToUse: Checking if a decomposition preserves all original information

terms.means: schemas of the decomposed relations

formulas.trap: Lossy decomposition means the join produces MORE tuples (spurious tuples), NOT fewer. The original is a SUBSET of the join result.

formulas.name: Tuple Relational Calculus (TRC)
formulas.plain: { t | P(t) } — set of all tuples t satisfying predicate P
formulas.whenToUse: Expressing queries declaratively using tuple variables and logical predicates

terms.means: tuple variable ranging over a relation
logical predicate involving t

formulas.trap: TRC can be unsafe (infinite results) if the domain is unrestricted. Safe TRC == domain-independent queries.

formulas.name: Domain Relational Calculus (DRC)
formulas.plain: { <x1,...,xn> | P(x1,...,xn) } — set of domain value tuples satisfying predicate P
formulas.whenToUse: Expressing queries using domain variables (each variable is a single attribute value)

terms.means: domain variable for one attribute value
predicate involving domain variables and relation membership

formulas.trap: DRC uses DOMAIN variables (single attribute), while TRC uses TUPLE variables (full row). Confusing them is a common GATE error.

formulas.name: Codd's Theorem (Equivalence)
formulas.plain: Relational Algebra is equivalent in expressive power to Safe TRC and Safe DRC
formulas.whenToUse: Questions about expressive power or comparing RA vs RC

terms.means: equivalent in expressive power (same set of queries expressible)

formulas.trap: Datalog with recursion is MORE powerful than RA; SQL with recursion (WITH RECURSIVE) is also more powerful. Pure RA cannot express transitive closure.

formulas.name: Existential and Universal Quantifiers in Calculus
formulas.plain: EXISTS t in R(P(t)): at least one tuple satisfies P; FORALL t in R(P(t)): every tuple satisfies P
formulas.whenToUse: Translating 'there exists' or 'for all' conditions in query logic

terms.means: existential quantifier — 'there exists at least one'
universal quantifier — 'for all / every'

formulas.trap: Universal quantification (for all) maps to division in RA; existential maps to a join/select. Mixing them up leads to wrong RA expressions.

formulas.name: SQL Query Structure
formulas.latex: \text{SELECT}\; A_1,\ldots,A_k \;\text{FROM}\; R_1,\ldots,R_m \;[\text{WHERE}\; P] \;[\text{GROUP BY}\; G] \;[\text{HAVING}\; H]
formulas.plain: SELECT cols FROM tables [WHERE pred] [GROUP BY cols] [HAVING pred]
formulas.whenToUse: Writing or interpreting any SQL SELECT query

terms.means: output attributes or aggregate expressions
grouping attributes
predicate on groups (applied after GROUP BY)

formulas.trap: HAVING filters GROUPS (after grouping); WHERE filters ROWS (before grouping). Using aggregate functions in WHERE is a syntax error.

formulas.name: SQL Aggregate Functions
formulas.plain: COUNT(*): all rows; COUNT(A): non-NULL values of A; SUM/AVG/MAX/MIN: over non-NULL values
formulas.whenToUse: Computing summary statistics on groups or the whole table

terms.means: counts all rows including NULLs
counts non-NULL values of attribute A

formulas.trap: COUNT(*) includes NULL rows; COUNT(col) excludes NULLs. SUM/AVG/MIN/MAX all ignore NULLs. A GATE question may test this difference.

formulas.name: NULL Semantics and Three-Valued Logic
formulas.latex: \text{NULL} \neq \text{NULL}; \quad x = \text{NULL} \Rightarrow \text{UNKNOWN}; \quad \text{Use IS NULL / IS NOT NULL}
formulas.plain: NULL compared to anything yields UNKNOWN (not TRUE/FALSE). Check with IS NULL, not = NULL.
formulas.whenToUse: Any query involving missing values, outer joins, or aggregation over nullable columns

terms.means: third truth value in SQL's three-valued logic (TRUE/FALSE/UNKNOWN)

formulas.trap: WHERE clause only passes rows where condition is TRUE (not UNKNOWN). So 'WHERE A = NULL' never returns rows — must use 'WHERE A IS NULL'.

formulas.name: EXISTS vs IN Subquery
formulas.plain: EXISTS: true if subquery is non-empty; IN: true if value equals some value from subquery
formulas.whenToUse: Correlated subqueries (EXISTS) and set membership checks (IN)

terms.means: correlated subquery returns at least one row
value is a member of the subquery result set

formulas.trap: NOT IN with NULLs in subquery returns UNKNOWN (no rows pass). NOT EXISTS handles NULLs correctly. Classic GATE trap.

formulas.name: SQL JOIN Syntax
formulas.latex: \text{INNER JOIN / LEFT [OUTER] JOIN / RIGHT [OUTER] JOIN / FULL [OUTER] JOIN / CROSS JOIN / NATURAL JOIN}
formulas.plain: INNER JOIN: matched rows only; LEFT JOIN: all left + NULLs; RIGHT JOIN: all right + NULLs; FULL JOIN: all rows both sides
formulas.whenToUse: Writing SQL joins that need to preserve unmatched rows from one or both tables

terms.means: explicit join condition
join on named common column (removes duplicate)

formulas.trap: NATURAL JOIN in SQL auto-joins on ALL columns with same name — adding a new column to a table can silently change query results.

formulas.name: SQL Set Operations
formulas.plain: UNION/INTERSECT/EXCEPT remove duplicates by default; add ALL to keep duplicates
formulas.whenToUse: Combining results of two SELECT statements with the same column types

terms.means: concatenates all rows including duplicates (faster)
rows in first SELECT not in second (equivalent to set difference)

formulas.trap: SQL UNION removes duplicates (like RA union) but UNION ALL does NOT. SQL SELECT by default keeps duplicates unlike RA projection.

formulas.name: DISTINCT vs No DISTINCT in SQL
formulas.latex: \text{SELECT } A \text{ FROM } R \; \leftrightarrow \; \text{multiset result}; \quad \text{SELECT DISTINCT } A \text{ FROM } R \; \leftrightarrow \; \Pi_A(R)
formulas.plain: SELECT without DISTINCT is a multiset (bag); SELECT DISTINCT gives set (= RA projection)
formulas.whenToUse: Understanding difference between SQL (bags) and RA (sets) semantics

terms.means: a set that allows duplicate elements (also called bag)

formulas.trap: RA projection always removes duplicates (set semantics). SQL without DISTINCT keeps duplicates (bag semantics). GATE exploits this difference.

formulas.name: ALL and ANY / SOME Quantifiers
formulas.plain: x > ALL(subquery): x greater than maximum value in subquery; x > ANY(subquery): x greater than at least one value
formulas.whenToUse: Comparing a value against all or some values returned by a subquery

terms.means: condition must hold for every value in subquery
condition must hold for at least one value in subquery

formulas.trap: x > ALL(empty set) is TRUE (vacuously). x > ANY(empty set) is FALSE. This edge case appears in GATE.

formulas.name: SQL Logical Order of Execution
formulas.plain: Execution order: FROM -> WHERE -> GROUP BY -> HAVING -> SELECT -> ORDER BY
formulas.whenToUse: Debugging or predicting results of complex SQL queries with filters and grouping

terms.means: executed AFTER WHERE/GROUP BY/HAVING — aliases not available in WHERE

formulas.trap: Column aliases defined in SELECT cannot be used in WHERE or HAVING (evaluated before SELECT). They can be used in ORDER BY.

formulas.name: Primary Key and Foreign Key Constraints
formulas.latex: \text{PRIMARY KEY} \Rightarrow \text{NOT NULL} + \text{UNIQUE}; \quad \text{FOREIGN KEY}(A) \text{ REFERENCES } R(B) \Rightarrow A \in \Pi_B(R) \cup \{\text{NULL}\}
formulas.plain: Primary key: NOT NULL + UNIQUE. Foreign key A must reference an existing value in R.B or be NULL.
formulas.whenToUse: Questions about integrity constraints, referential integrity, or insertion/deletion anomalies

terms.means: referential integrity constraint between tables

formulas.trap: A foreign key CAN be NULL (unless also declared NOT NULL). A primary key CANNOT be NULL — even partial NULL in composite PK is disallowed.

formulas.name: SQL Views
formulas.latex: \text{CREATE VIEW } V \text{ AS } Q; \quad \text{Updatable iff: single table, no DISTINCT/GROUP BY/HAVING/aggregates, no subqueries in WHERE}
formulas.plain: View is a named query. Updatable view: single base table, no aggregates, no GROUP BY, no DISTINCT.
formulas.whenToUse: Questions about view definition, updatability, or virtual relation behavior

terms.means: view name (virtual table)
defining SQL query

formulas.trap: Views defined with GROUP BY or aggregates are NOT updatable. Materialized views store the result physically, ordinary views do not.
